column1: # Fiber Reinforced Polyurethane FRPU Laser Cleaning Dataset
# Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab

# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Fiber Reinforced Polyurethane FRPU Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/composite/fiber-reinforced/fiber-reinforced-polyurethane-frpu-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for FRPU cleaning without thermal damage to fiber reinforcement
Near-IR wavelength optimized for polyurethane absorption while minimizing fiber damage
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on FRPU surface
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning with minimal thermal accumulation
Fluence threshold for effective polyurethane removal while preserving fibers
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of polyurethane matrix
Optimal scanning speed for efficient coverage without excessive dwell time
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal input

Typical range for polymer composites like FRPU to balance cleaning efficiency and avoid thermal degradation; starting at 100W for light contamination. Max safe ~150-200W before substrate melting (polyurethane degrades ~200°C). Source: IPG Photonics fiber laser specs for cleaning; studies on polymer ablation (e.g., J. Appl. Phys. 2018).

Effective for contamination removal on low-conductivity FRPU; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and Adapt-Laser manuals for composite cleaning.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of polymers; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but overkill for FRPU. 50 ns minimizes heat-affected zone while ablating contaminants. Source: Laser physics research on polymer ablation (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

Typical for pulsed fiber lasers in cleaning; 40 kHz recommended for FRPU to enable fast processing without excessive heat accumulation in low-thermal-conductivity material. Higher rates (>60 kHz) risk buildup. Source: Academic literature on composite laser processing (Composites Part B, 2019).

Fluence range for safe contaminant removal on FRPU; damage threshold ~1.5-2.5 J/cm² due to polyurethane's low melting point (~180-250°C) and moderate 1064 nm absorption. Recommended 0.8 J/cm² for cleaning without substrate harm. Source: Ablation threshold studies (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2017).

Beam diameter for precise cleaning on FRPU surfaces; smaller (50-100 μm) for fine details, larger (200-300 μm) for broad areas. 150 μm recommended for uniform coverage in industrial prep. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-4) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete FRPU cleaning to ensure removal without overexposure. Source: Industry case studies on polymer composite cleaning (J. Clean. Prod. 2021).

Ensures uniform coverage on irregular FRPU surfaces; 60% recommended for even cleaning without gaps or excessive overlap-induced heat. Typical 30-80% range. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2019).
